q: What are connector policies (policy templates) used for?
o: To transform requests/responses at runtime without writing code
why: Policy templates modify traffic (headers, values, routing) declaratively, code-free.

q: How can you apply and manage connector policies besides the UI?
o: Using the CLI to view and modify them
why: Policies can be viewed and modified via the CLI in addition to the maker UI.

q: Decide whether each policy statement is true.

rows.s: Policies can read runtime values via policy expressions.
Multiple policies can be applied to a connector.

why: Policies use expressions on runtime values and can be layered, all without plug-ins.

q: A policy that needs the value of an incoming header would use...
o: A policy expression referencing the header
why: Policy expressions access runtime values such as headers, query and body.

q: Policy expressions can access which runtime values?
o: Headers, query string, body and connection values
why: Expressions can read headers, query parameters, request body and connection values.

q: Which is a valid use of a data-conversion policy?
o: Reshaping or converting request/response data formats
why: Data-conversion policies transform payload data formats between client and backend.

q: Decide whether each expression statement is true.

rows.s: Policies can read a value from the connection (e.g. a stored setting).
Policies can work with query string values.

why: Policy expressions can reference connection values and query string parameters.

q: Working with the response body in a policy lets you...
o: Transform or extract data returned by the backend
why: Response-body policies transform/extract data returned to the caller.

q: Which policy changes where the request is actually sent?
why: Host URL and route-request policies redirect the outgoing request.

q: The 'Set property' policy template is used to...
o: Add or update a value in the request/response
why: Set property adds or updates values within the message.

q: Decide whether each routing/values statement is true.

rows.s: You can set or update a query string parameter with a policy template.
Routing policies can send the call to a different host.
Policies cannot add values to a request.

why: Templates set query strings and values and can reroute to a different host.

q: The 'Set query string parameter' policy template lets you...
o: Add or modify a query parameter on outgoing requests
why: It injects/updates a query string parameter on the request.

q: Which is the biggest advantage of using policies vs custom code for these transforms?
o: No code to maintain; configured declaratively on the connector
They remove the need for auth
why: Policies are declarative/no-code, reducing maintenance versus custom transform code.

q: A backend expects an extra header your callers don't send. Which approach fits best?
o: A policy that sets the header on the request
why: A set-value policy can inject the required header automatically.

q: Decide whether each closing policy statement is true.

rows.s: Policies can transform both requests and responses.
Policy expressions enable dynamic, runtime-driven behavior.

why: Policies act on requests and responses, driven dynamically by expressions.

q: To apply a policy you generally...
o: Add the policy template to the connector and configure its parameters
why: You add a policy template to the connector and set its parameters (no backend changes).
